claims.claim: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive language model inference by drafting candidate token sequences and verifying them in a single forward pass, but conventional approaches require a separate neural draft model that consumes additional GPU VRAM—a binding constraint for local and consumer-hardware deployments.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: In proxy benchmarks—a continuation-oracle matching test and greedy verification against distilgpt2—the context-derived trie achieves mean accepted draft lengths of 2.0–2.4 tokens per step on local Python stdlib and system documentation corpora, with first-token acceptance rates of 0.43–0.48.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: A synthetic repetition control confirms the mechanism recovers repeated continuations perfectly (8.0 accepted tokens/step, 1.0 acceptance rate).

claims.claim: Speculative decoding reduces the latency of autoregressive generation by proposing short token sequences (drafts) that a target model verifies in a single batched forward pass.
claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: When the target accepts draft tokens, multiple positions advance per forward call, amortizing the per-token cost of the verifier.

claims.claim: For deployments on consumer GPUs where VRAM is the primary constraint, the draft model's memory footprint can make speculative decoding infeasible even when it would improve throughput.

claims.claim: Negligible draft latency.** Trie lookups are O(1) per token, orders of magnitude faster than a neural forward pass.

claims.claim: Given a context window of tokens $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_T$, we construct a suffix trie recording all n-gram continuations observed in the context for $n$ up to a maximum order $N$ (default $N = 8$).
